verses.text: Joseph reports to Pharaoh: my father and brothers have arrived with their flocks and herds; they are in Goshen. He brings five of his brothers in and presents them to Pharaoh — a careful diplomatic introduction.

Joseph reports to Pharaoh: my father and brothers have arrived with their flocks and herds; they are in Goshen. He brings five of his brothers in and presents them to Pharaoh — a careful diplomatic introduction.

What is your occupation? Pharaoh asks. They answer as Joseph had coached: we are shepherds, like our fathers. We have come because of the famine in Canaan; let your servants dwell in Goshen. The honest answer also positions them at the social distance they need.

What is your occupation? Pharaoh asks. They answer as Joseph had coached: we are shepherds, like our fathers. We have come because of the famine in Canaan; let your servants dwell in Goshen. The honest answer also positions them at the social distance they need.

Pharaoh tells Joseph: the land of Egypt is before you. Settle your father and brothers in the best of the land — Goshen will do. If any of them are capable, make them overseers of my own livestock. The pagan king extends genuine hospitality, not mere tolerance.

Pharaoh tells Joseph: the land of Egypt is before you. Settle your father and brothers in the best of the land — Goshen will do. If any of them are capable, make them overseers of my own livestock. The pagan king extends genuine hospitality, not mere tolerance.

Joseph brings his father Jacob in and sets him before Pharaoh. Jacob blesses Pharaoh. Hebrews would later note that the lesser is blessed by the greater — Jacob, an old shepherd, blesses the king of the world's superpower. Pharaoh asks: how old are you?

Joseph brings his father Jacob in and sets him before Pharaoh. Jacob blesses Pharaoh. Hebrews would later note that the lesser is blessed by the greater — Jacob, an old shepherd, blesses the king of the world's superpower. Pharaoh asks: how old are you?

The days of the years of my pilgrimage are one hundred and thirty, says Jacob — few and evil, and they have not reached the days of my fathers' pilgrimage. He sums up his whole life in two short, sober sentences. Then he blesses Pharaoh again, and goes out.

The days of the years of my pilgrimage are one hundred and thirty, says Jacob — few and evil, and they have not reached the days of my fathers' pilgrimage. He sums up his whole life in two short, sober sentences. Then he blesses Pharaoh again, and goes out.

Joseph settles his father and brothers in the best of Egypt, in the land of Rameses, just as Pharaoh said. He provides bread for them all, according to the size of each family. Nothing is left to chance for those he loves.

Joseph settles his father and brothers in the best of Egypt, in the land of Rameses, just as Pharaoh said. He provides bread for them all, according to the size of each family. Nothing is left to chance for those he loves.

Meanwhile, no bread is in any land; the famine is fierce. Egypt and Canaan grow faint. Joseph gathers all the money in both lands for the grain they buy, and brings it into Pharaoh's house. When the money runs out, the Egyptians come to Joseph: give us bread — why should we die before you?

Meanwhile, no bread is in any land; the famine is fierce. Egypt and Canaan grow faint. Joseph gathers all the money in both lands for the grain they buy, and brings it into Pharaoh's house. When the money runs out, the Egyptians come to Joseph: give us bread — why should we die before you?

Meanwhile, no bread is in any land; the famine is fierce. Egypt and Canaan grow faint. Joseph gathers all the money in both lands for the grain they buy, and brings it into Pharaoh's house. When the money runs out, the Egyptians come to Joseph: give us bread — why should we die before you?

Joseph answers: give me your livestock. They bring him horses, flocks, herds, donkeys, and he feeds them through that year. Joseph turns Egypt's wealth from cash to herds to land, a chapter at a time, but always with food in their hands.

Joseph answers: give me your livestock. They bring him horses, flocks, herds, donkeys, and he feeds them through that year. Joseph turns Egypt's wealth from cash to herds to land, a chapter at a time, but always with food in their hands.

The next year they return: our money is gone, our cattle are yours; nothing is left but our bodies and our lands. Why should we die before you? Buy us and our land for bread. Let us be Pharaoh's servants, only give us seed to plant. They volunteer their freedom for survival.

The next year they return: our money is gone, our cattle are yours; nothing is left but our bodies and our lands. Why should we die before you? Buy us and our land for bread. Let us be Pharaoh's servants, only give us seed to plant. They volunteer their freedom for survival.

So Joseph buys all the land of Egypt for Pharaoh. Every Egyptian sells his field; the land becomes Pharaoh's. Only the land of the priests Joseph does not buy — they receive their food from the king and did not need to sell. A pagan priesthood is exempted; God's plan moves around their land.

So Joseph buys all the land of Egypt for Pharaoh. Every Egyptian sells his field; the land becomes Pharaoh's. Only the land of the priests Joseph does not buy — they receive their food from the king and did not need to sell. A pagan priesthood is exempted; God's plan moves around their land.

So Joseph buys all the land of Egypt for Pharaoh. Every Egyptian sells his field; the land becomes Pharaoh's. Only the land of the priests Joseph does not buy — they receive their food from the king and did not need to sell. A pagan priesthood is exempted; God's plan moves around their land.

Joseph tells the people: I have bought you and your land for Pharaoh today. Here is seed — sow the land. At harvest, give a fifth to Pharaoh; the other four-fifths are yours, for seed, food, your households, and your children. The people answer: you have saved our lives. Let us be Pharaoh's servants. This twenty-percent tax became Egyptian law.

Joseph tells the people: I have bought you and your land for Pharaoh today. Here is seed — sow the land. At harvest, give a fifth to Pharaoh; the other four-fifths are yours, for seed, food, your households, and your children. The people answer: you have saved our lives. Let us be Pharaoh's servants. This twenty-percent tax became Egyptian law.

Joseph tells the people: I have bought you and your land for Pharaoh today. Here is seed — sow the land. At harvest, give a fifth to Pharaoh; the other four-fifths are yours, for seed, food, your households, and your children. The people answer: you have saved our lives. Let us be Pharaoh's servants. This twenty-percent tax became Egyptian law.

Joseph tells the people: I have bought you and your land for Pharaoh today. Here is seed — sow the land. At harvest, give a fifth to Pharaoh; the other four-fifths are yours, for seed, food, your households, and your children. The people answer: you have saved our lives. Let us be Pharaoh's servants. This twenty-percent tax became Egyptian law.

Meanwhile Israel dwells in Goshen; they take possession and grow exceedingly. Jacob lives seventeen more years in Egypt — the same number of years Joseph had lived under his father before being sold. He is one hundred and forty-seven when his life closes in.

Meanwhile Israel dwells in Goshen; they take possession and grow exceedingly. Jacob lives seventeen more years in Egypt — the same number of years Joseph had lived under his father before being sold. He is one hundred and forty-seven when his life closes in.

When the time draws near for Israel to die, he calls Joseph: if I have found grace with you, put your hand under my thigh and swear — do not bury me in Egypt. Lay me with my fathers, in the land. I will do as you have said. Swear to me. Joseph swears. And Israel bowed himself upon the head of his bed — even now, worshipping.

I will do as you have said. Swear to me. Joseph swears. And Israel bowed himself upon the head of his bed — even now, worshipping.
